sentences.text: Well, the train was our first introduction to concentration camps.
Actually, we were stunned.
That's the best way I can put it.
Now, we were hardened combat veterans at that time.
We'd been in combat almost two years.
Our unit spent more time in combat than any other unit or division-- than any other unit of the United States Army, with the exception of the 3rd Infantry Division.
So we were used to death.
I mean, death was a constant companion.
But we had never visioned anything of this magnitude, of a couple of thousand dead civilians herded into boxcars and kept there until they died.
It stunned us.
That-- we had a difficult time believing it.
And not much was said.
I couldn't think of a thing to say.
And I'm not one for-- lacks in words.
About every then there-- began intimidation cursing heard people talking about getting those dirty Nazi dogs.
And, and some men got furious.
And I saw a few who, who broke down and were crying.
[CRYING] Well, we searched all the cars and kind of came to our senses.
And that's when I gave the order then.
Let's go into the camp.
So the main body of I Company and the company commander followed the tracks, which went into the-- to the camp itself.
And it was a big gate, there.
It was locked with a padlock.
And one of the men shot the padlock off with his rifle.
And I went with the right platoon, which I found out-- we went in, in what we call a skirmish formation.
That is, we-- the two lead platoons fan out.
And lead scouts go forward from each platoon.
Well, I went with the right platoon, which did not follow the track.
We found ourselves faced with a masonry-- high masonry wall.
Well, I couldn't-- it was hard to scramble over.
And I, I left my radio and my radio operator and my jeep-- at that time, I was in a jeep.
Or I had been.
And so I left them outside the camp.
And so I did not have my radio when I went in.
What I had was my rifleman.
There was always a rifleman with me.
He was sort of my bodyguard.
And I had an interpreter, a German interpreter.
That is, he was an American soldier.
But he was born in Germany.
He was a young American soldier.
He Migrated to Germany.
His father was a professor at some college in Germany.
And he did not like Hitler.
So he got out while he could.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I think the soldier was, like, 10 years old when he came to the States because he spoke obviously fluent German.
Hmm?
No.
He was not Jewish.
His name was Karl Mann.
And he was about 19 years old at the time, just out of high school.
And he was with me all the time-- and the rifleman.
The three of us were always right together because I had-- an interpreter, you know, was invaluable to me.
And the rifleman I used also to carry messages or to give orders back and forth.
And we climbed over the wall, following the right patroon, which would fan out on a long skirmish line.
And each man went over.
Now, we did not receive any fire from the Germans, which-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, I really wasn't anticipating much of a battle there.
As I had it then-- with a reinforced company, I had about 200 men, a little over 200 men.
But the post was very large.
It covered well over a hundred acres-- I guess several hundred acres.
And when we first got into it, what we found-- the left unit, with the company commander, they got into warehouse areas and maintenance buildings.
There were a great number of buildings in there.
There were barracks, supply-- great stores of supplies.
And when I went over the wall, I found myself in the back of a-- of a very beautiful home, which I immediately assumed were officers' homes.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I yelled at my two men, be careful of booby traps!
We did not encounter any, I might add, at any time in Dachau.
But I went in the back door of this home with my two men.
And I looked around.
And we walked in carefully and walked through the house.
It was empty.
But in one bedroom, I found children's toys scattered on the floor and great signs of disarray, like hasty packing had been done.
And they-- this family, this German family-- officer family had left.
So looking at that, I knew immediately that probably many of the officers or most of them had fled the area.
These were all SS officers.
The concentration camps were strictly SS people and Gestapo.
So I immediately assumed that when I saw this hasty evacuation that the camp had been at least partially evacuated.
So we walked in carefully-- and I went out the front door of this home.
And I found myself in a beautifully landscaped area.
It was like a small city, you know, paved roads, flower-- roses were blooming or starting to bloom, as I recall.
The grass was pretty green.
And it was just a very nice, residential area.
At that point, I heard some firing off to my left.
And I recognized a small arms fire and no automatic weapons.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then I pressed forward with my group, following this platoon.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we went very slowly because searching all those buildings took a great deal of time.
We were extremely cautious.
But soldiers entered every building and searched it-- the building.
Then they started finding German soldiers-- hiding out, mostly, in those various buildings.
They were hiding in there.
So I knew-- I further realized that there was not going to be much opposition, if any.
We went to-- we didn't search the officers' homes, except the one I went through.
But when we got past those, we found the barracks where the SS troops slept.
And we found their headquarters building, the post headquarters, a very beautiful building.
And I would-- as I say, I was following this right platoon at a safe distance behind them.
I didn't want to be the lead rifleman.
And I came to the headquarters building.
And I went inside of it and looked around.
And it was empty.
And then I heard more firing and-- to my front.
And I immediately left.
And right after I left, we picked up our first prisoner that I saw.
It was a man in a striped uniform.
He was hiding out.
He came out with his hands up.
And I saw immediately that he was a prisoner.
He was not a German soldier.
So I told my interpreter can-- see if you can communicate with him.
He could.
This guy could speak German.
But it turned out he was Polish.
But he could speak pretty good German.
I guess he'd been a prisoner for some time.
Well, I said, you tell him to stay right with me.
I want him to explain to me where we are and what's going on.
So this guy stayed with us.
And I moved forward very slowly.
And there was sporadic firing, not a lot, but sporadic.
Whether the Germans actually did any shooting, I do not know.
My men claim that they were fired upon.
But in my opinion, they were not.
But I couldn't-- vision was difficult because of the many buildings.
Everywhere I looked, there were buildings in my way.
And we didn't see the prison camp itself.
I don't know where the prisoners were.
I assumed that there were prisoners somewhere because what this guy had picked up.
So I kept going forward very slowly.
Then I came to a point where there was an L-shaped cement structure that had been used as a coal bin.
It was open.
It had no top on it.
But it had a wall about 8 or 9 feet high on two sides of it-- L-shaped.
And I could see what was left of-- there were coal dust on the ground.
So I knew it had been used, then, as a, a sort of a storage place for coal.
And there was a small railroad track running into that area, a narrow-gauge track.
And I saw what, to me-- I'm from a mining country-- was an old mining car, an ore-mining car.
And I immediately realized that they had used that little track to haul coal from wherever it was brought in and then dump it in that area.
And the track ran right along the area.
At that time when I got there, I Company had collected a number of prisoners.
And I put them up in that little area.
And there was probably 50 or 60 German prisoners-- German soldiers.
And they had two or three men guarding them.
And one man was on a machine gun.
One of my men had a light machine gun mounted, aimed at this-- where the prisoners lined up against this wall.
Well, I watched it for a couple, three minutes.
And one of the I Company men came up to me and said, Colonel, he said, you better-- you, you better see what we found.
So I started to follow him off.
And I had my back turned when I heard the light machine gun go off.
I was probably 40, 50 feet away at that time.
I wheel around.
And the machine gunner let loose a burst of probably 20 rounds.
We always fired machine guns in bursts to keep from burning the gun up.
And he fired a, a continuous burst of probably about 20 rounds and stopped.
Well, at that time, I was running towards him.
And he had his back towards me.
And I kicked him in the back with my boot and grabbed him by the collar.
He was a small man.
And I yanked him backwards.
And I said, what the hell are you doing?
He, he started crying.
And he said, well, Colonel, they were trying to get away.
Well, they were milling around.
But they weren't trying to-- in my opinion, they weren't trying to get away.
Well, I looked over the scene.
And the Germans dived on the ground-- to the ground when he opened up.
When I turned around, they were hitting the ground.
And I couldn't tell just exactly how many he had killed or shot.
Anyway, I yanked him off the gun.
And I yelled to another man.
He's-- whoever the sergeant there.
I said, Sergeant, put somebody else on this gun.
At the same time, when I ran over there, I drew my pistol-- my .45-- and fired it into the air several times to get everybody's attention.
And I was yelling, there'll be no more firing unless I give a specific order.
Well, the damage was done.
There was a small hospital unit right behind this wall.
They had-- I could see the red cross on the-- on the top of the building.
So we checked out the men that had been shot.
There were about 11 that were dead.
Some others were wounded.
And we took them into that hospital.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there was a German doctor there and a German medical crew.
Well, then I ordered the company commander to move all of the prisoners out of the post as rapidly as possible.
So they rounded up then what was left of them.
There was 11 dead and 7 or 8 that were wounded.
But the rest of them were all right.
They got up.
And we marched them off.
Hmm?
These were German soldiers.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then I got that thing halfway under-- what I thought was more or less under control.
I heard some firing then ahead of me.
Well, I couldn't see where it was.
But I went then pretty rapidly.
I didn't waste any time.
And I came out-- then, for the first time, I saw the prison area.
Up to that time, it had been obscured by the buildings.
I could not see it.
It was on the far, extreme corner of the whole post-- occupied only a small portion of the entire garrison area.
It was just a, a small-- well, it was a confinement area.
Maybe it was a mile all the way around-- a-- around the thing.
But it was a fairly substantial area.
And then I could see-- when I first got to where I could see it, I was right next to a place that I found was a crematorium.
And I looked in there.
And they had bodies stacked in a small, cement room next to the crematorium.
These were nude bodies.
And were stacked like cordwood in that building-- in that little, small building.
And next to it was a crematorium that had four, four coal-fired furnaces.
The furnaces were not operating when we got there, and I don't think had been operating for several days because they were cold.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I surveyed that for a minute or two.
And then I went out to follow the path along the wall.
The-- I saw the confinement was a-- it was a high, barbed-wire fence surrounding the area.
And there was a guard tower about every 200 yards.
And there was huge cement guard towers with a machine gun and a small, German crew at each guard tower.
Well, I came across the first guard tower.
And there were five German soldiers lying near the base of the tower, which I immediately assumed had been the crew that was in that guard tower.
Well, I assumed that my men had killed them.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then I walked on down-- one of the-- one of the I Company men said, Colonel, the entrance to this is right down here.
Well, it was about a hundred yards from the guard tower.
And I followed along.
There was a, a-- I was just immediately outside of the cement-- of the barbed-wire fence was a path maybe 4 feet wide that led to all of the guard towers.
And outside of the path was a moat.
I call it a 'moat' because it was a-- had been constructed and held water.
And the water came from the Amper River, which ran through the, the city of Dachau.
And it was-- it was filled with water.
I was on the outside of the path.
Well, I followed along the edge of that moat.
And there was a line of trees all the way along that moat.
And I got down then to-- when I saw the entrance to the prison area.
And there was a building or several buildings right at the entrance.
It was one building that bridged the entrance.
And I immediately assumed that was-- what we, in American parlance would call-- a guardhouse.
It's where the guards slept on their-- on shift.
The Germans call it a [GERMAN], I found out later.
Well, I was standing there.
All of a sudden-- at that time, the first thing I saw was several hundred bodies lying inside in an open space near the-- this gate to the prison area.
And there were several hundred dead people lying there.
Most had been stripped nude, no clothing on them.
And I, I didn't see any live people in there at that, that moment.
But just a few moments later, the whole place erupted.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the prisoners started streaming out of the barracks.
I could see a large number of barracks-- small, one-story barracks inside the prison compound that housed the prisoners.
Well, they came streaming out.
They had finally realized what was going on.
And they screamed.
And they shouted.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they started tossing bodies through the air.
And I was just stunned.
I-- for a moment, I couldn't figure out what was going on.
Because there were thousands of prisoners.
It turned out there was about almost 32,000 in there.
And the ones that were still able to walk around [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were tossing these bodies over their outstretched hands.
And they were tearing at them with their hands.
And I immediately said to my interpreter, ask this guy, this prisoner who is with us, what's going on in there?
They talked in German for a few seconds.
And the-- my interpreter said, well, Colonel, he said they're killing the informers.
That's the word he used to me.
Well, we were helpless to do anything about it.
But it quieted down.
And I said to start yelling at them in German and tell them that they have to remain here.
And we'll get food, medicine, up to them as rapidly as we can.
But they are not to try to get out of the compound.
I couldn't-- I could see I didn't want to have prisoners running all over the area.
We were still in combat.
Besides, I-- most of them were desperately ill.
So it quieted down.
And I was yelling.
The company then, we started encircling the whole area and taking over the guard towers.
And it got very quiet there for a few minutes.
And the prisoners were just-- inside the compound, were just standing there [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very listlessly.
And I was yelling, don't throw them any food.
I don't want to-- I figured they'd fight over it.
Well it quieted down for a moment.
And all of a sudden-- I'm standing near the entrance to the prison area.
There was a small cement bridge that went over the moat.
And then there was a gate-- a double gate that opened into the prison compound.
It was closed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But it was not locked.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I'm standing there surveying the, the scene.
And suddenly, three jeeps come up to the bridge.
There was a-- General Linden from the 42nd Division was in the lead jeep.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he said, Colonel, I have a, a lady reporter here.
She wants to interview these prisoners.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, I said, she can't go in there.
I says, we're not going to open that gate.
Because the people were pressed up against the gate.
They want out.
She can't go in there.
And I said, my orders are not to let anyone in or out.
And that's what I'm doing.
Well, he said, I'll take the responsibility for it.
I says, General, you're not in your area.
You're out of your combat zone.
This is my area.
And I take my orders from my commanding general.
Well, he said, I see no harm in it.
And while I'm talking to him, this woman gets out of the jeep-- second jeep, and runs up to the gate, and opens it.
Well, pandemonium broke loose there.
The prisoners surged forward.
Then I ordered my men to start firing over their heads and rush that gate and close it.
They did.
They start firing their rifles into the air.
And about half of them rushed up to the gate and got it closed.
The woman got frightened in the meantime.
And she ran back.
Well, before she went in there, she said to me, she said, Colonel, I, I want to interview some important prisoners.
She said-- Cardinal Niemoller, she said.
Well, I'd never heard of Cardinal Niemoller.
And the Prince of Luxembourg-- and she gave one other name that I didn't catch.
Well, I said, I don't know anything about them.
But you can't go in there.
And, while I'm talking to the general, she did anyway.
Well, she ran back after the firing started and got in her jeep.
And then the general and I got into a pretty heated argument.
He said, I'm relieving you of command.
And I'm taking responsibility.
I says, no.
You-- this is my area.
You're not relieving me of command.
So finally, I said to one of the privates standing there, I said, Private, escort this general and his party out of here.
They had come in the main gate to the-- to the whole-- to the whole compound-- to the whole area, which is about two blocks from this prison gate.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] well, the private stepped forward.
He didn't know what what the hell to do.
And this general carried a little, old stick, I don't know, a swagger stick or riding crop [POINTING] about that long with him.
And he was a rather short guy, a little pudgy.
And as the private approached the jeep, he whacked him over the helmet with his stick.
Well, it had already been a long, trying day.
And I exploded at that point.
I hauled out my .45.
I said, you son of a bitch, if you touch another one of my men, I'll kill you right here.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He was standing up in the front of his jeep when he hit the, the soldier.
He sat down.
And in the third jeep, which I hadn't paid much attention to, there was a-- two lieutenant colonels on it.
One was the commanding officer of the 22nd Infantry Battalion of the 42nd Infantry Division.
The other one was at battalion commander.
This guy, the battalion commander of the name of [?
Flintz, ?] he came running up to me.
And he said, you can't talk to my general like that.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He said, I'll see you after the war.
I said, well, you son of a bitch, what's wrong with right now?
And he went back and got in his jeep.
And his party left.
But as he left, the general said, I'll see you before a general court martial.
Well, that was the last thing that was worrying me at that point.
They left, anyway.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
And then things got fairly normal.
Who was the reporter?
What was her name?
Her name was Marguerite Higgins.
She represented a newspaper in New York. 'Maggie Higgins' they called her-- very attractive young lady.
She had on, like, army fatigues.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Anyway, they left.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And a short time later, our commanding general arrived, General Frederick.
And I told him what happened.
It went, don't worry about it.
I'll take care of it.
And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at that time, I had halted my other companies in the attack.
So we got things quieted down.
And I got word that they would send up some army hospital as rapidly as they could.

And the-- I got word then from my regimental commander that one of the companies from the reserve battalion, which was our first battalion at that time, would be up to relieve my company so that I could get-- pull my company out and reassemble the battalion to resume the attack into Munich the next morning.
This general came in somewhere around 3:30-- and his party-- that evening.
So I got word that C Company would be up to relieve me.
And shortly thereafter, C Company arrived.
Well, I had men scattered all over that area.
And so we were trying-- I was trying to extricate my men.
And the C Company commander came up.
And he established his headquarters in that hospital.
A little hospital, and took over the guarding.
Because all we were doing was guarding.
What we were doing was keeping the prisoners in.
That was our mission, to not let them get out and run all over the countryside until somebody relieved us.
